scope: New contributions in Iteration 7 not covered by Iter 6 novelty check. Three new contributions assessed: (1) SPWD rank velocity feedback WD, (2) Theorem 3 RG beta function dual derivation, (3) Proposition 1 alignment noise design constraint. Iter 6 scores (Theorem 1: 9/10, Theorem 2: 8/10, Theorem 3/PMP-WD: 8/10) maintained unchanged.

candidates.title: SPWD: Spectral-Phase-Transition Weight Decay (Rank Velocity Feedback)

collisions.paper: He et al., 2025. 'AlphaDecay: Module-wise Weight Decay for Heavy-Tailed Balancing in LLMs.' arXiv:2506.14562. NeurIPS 2025.
collisions.overlap: Both use per-layer spectral signals to modulate WD strength during training. Both are layer-wise adaptive WD methods.
collisions.notes: AlphaDecay uses STATIC spectral density (PL_Alpha_Hill from ESD snapshot). SPWD uses DYNAMIC rank velocity (temporal derivative of stable rank, d[||W||_F^2/||W||_2^2]/dt). These are orthogonal dimensions: AlphaDecay measures spectral shape; SPWD measures rate of structural change. A layer can have high PL_Alpha_Hill (heavy-tailed spectrum) but zero rank velocity (rank converged), or vice versa. Must differentiate explicitly in Related Work.

collisions.paper: Yunis et al., 2024. 'Approaching Deep Learning through the Spectral Dynamics of Weights.' arXiv:2408.11804.
collisions.overlap: Both track rank dynamics and spectral evolution during training. Both link WD to rank minimization behavior. Uses 'phase transition' language consistent with SPWD.
collisions.notes: Observational/descriptive paper only. No algorithmic proposal for rank velocity as WD feedback signal. Key citation for SPWD theoretical background.

collisions.paper: Shin et al., 2025. 'Dynamic Rank Adjustment for Accurate and Efficient Neural Network Training.' arXiv:2508.08625. Oct 2025.
collisions.overlap: Both involve dynamic rank tracking as a training signal.
collisions.notes: Adjusts model architecture (LoRA rank), not weight decay coefficient. Different control variable entirely. No WD modulation.

collisions.paper: arXiv:2502.17340. 'Low-rank Bias, Weight Decay, and Model Merging in Neural Networks.' Feb 2025.
collisions.overlap: Uses same stable rank definition (||W||_F/||W||_2). Confirms WD promotes stable rank reduction.
collisions.notes: Observational analysis of stable rank dynamics under WD. No algorithmic proposal for rank velocity feedback.

collisions.paper: arXiv:2507.12709. 'From SGD to Spectra: A Theory of Neural Network Weight Dynamics.' ICML 2025.
collisions.overlap: SDE framework connecting SGD to singular-value spectral dynamics. Provides theoretical foundation for why spectral dynamics evolve.
collisions.notes: Theoretical analysis only. Actually supports SPWD: singular value velocity appears naturally in the Dyson Brownian motion framework. Can cite as theoretical grounding.

candidates.differentiation_notes: SPWD is the first WD method using temporal derivative of stable rank (rank velocity) as continuous feedback signal. Must contrast explicitly with AlphaDecay: 'AlphaDecay assigns per-module WD based on static PL_Alpha_Hill snapshot; SPWD uses dynamic rank velocity v_t = d(stable_rank)/dt for continuous feedback.' Rank velocity detects when a layer is actively undergoing phase transition (rank collapse), enabling responsive WD modulation at critical moments. Computational advantage: requires only ||W||_F and ||W||_2 per layer (two scalars), cheaper than cosine similarity (O(d) inner product).

candidates.title: Theorem 3 RG Beta Function Dual Derivation (QA-WD as Remark 3.1 / Appendix)

collisions.paper: Atanasov, Zavatone-Veth, Pehlevan. 'Scaling and Renormalization in High-Dimensional Regression.' arXiv:2405.00592. 2024.
collisions.overlap: Applies RG techniques in ML context. Establishes RG-ML correspondence for high-dimensional regression scaling laws.
collisions.notes: Applied to regression generalization scaling, not WD scheduling. Does not derive any lambda*_t formula. Cite as background for RG-ML correspondence.

candidates.differentiation_notes: No prior work derives a WD feedback law from RG beta function theory. The convergence result (stochastic PMP via Riccati + RG beta function fixed point -> same proportional-feedback-on-gradient-weight-geometry formula) is novel. Must be presented modestly as Remark 3.1 / Appendix, not as a separate primary theorem. Acknowledge that the equivalence holds only in the moderate-alignment regime (delta_hat in [0.3, 0.7]). Present as 'two independent mathematical frameworks validate the same functional form for optimal WD feedback.'

candidates.title: Proposition 1: Alignment Noise Design Constraint (EMA Aggregation Requirement for WD Adaptation)

collisions.paper: Qin et al., 2021. 'SimiGrad: Fine-Grained Adaptive Batching for Large Scale Training using Gradient Similarity Measurement.' NeurIPS 2021.
collisions.overlap: Shows that gradient cosine similarity is nearly 0 (pure noise) at batch size 4, and that similarity stabilizes at very large batch sizes (65k+). Establishes empirical basis for gradient alignment noise at small batches.
collisions.notes: SimiGrad uses gradient similarity for adaptive batching (changing batch size). Proposition 1 uses the same empirical observation to derive a design constraint for WD adaptation at a fixed small batch size (b=128). SimiGrad never addresses weight decay scheduling or minimum aggregation horizon for alignment-based WD. Cite as empirical foundation for Proposition 1.

collisions.paper: McCandlish et al. 'An Empirical Model of Large-Batch Training.' arXiv:1812.06162. OpenAI.
collisions.overlap: Gradient noise scale (GNS) characterizes gradient noise as a function of batch size.
collisions.notes: Does not address alignment-aware WD or EMA aggregation horizon. Background citation only.

collisions.paper: Compagnoni et al. 'Adaptive Methods through the Lens of SDEs.' arXiv:2411.15958. 2024.
collisions.overlap: Noise analysis in adaptive gradient methods via SDE lens.
collisions.notes: Not focused on gradient cosine similarity or WD adaptation. Background citation only.

candidates.differentiation_notes: No prior paper characterizes a minimum aggregation horizon (k >= 10 EMA steps) for reliable alignment-aware WD adaptation at small batch sizes. SimiGrad establishes the empirical observation (similarity noise at small batch) but makes no connection to WD adaptation. The novelty is the specific application: CV(delta_hat_t) > 3 at b=128 -> EMA with k>=10 reduces CV < 1 -> single-step alignment-based WD is unreliable -> design constraint for all alignment-aware WD algorithms. Positive framing: alignment IS informative when aggregated.

candidates.title: Theorem 3 / PMP-WD: Pontryagin Optimal WD (maintained from Iter 6, strengthened by dual derivation)
candidates.differentiation_notes: Score maintained from Iter 6 (8/10). Strengthened by RG beta function dual derivation (Remark 3.1). No new competing papers found that threaten the state-feedback vs. feedforward distinction. See Iter 6 novelty_report.json for full prior art.

competitive_threat_update.reason: Uses per-layer spectral signal for WD modulation — same category as SPWD. Reviewers familiar with AlphaDecay will immediately ask 'what is the difference?' Must have a clear, concise answer in the Related Work section: static spectral density (AlphaDecay) vs. dynamic rank velocity (SPWD).
competitive_threat_update.mitigation: One-paragraph explicit comparison in Related Work. The temporal derivative / dynamic nature of SPWD is the key differentiator.

competitive_threat_update.reason: Provides SDE-based theory of spectral dynamics that actually supports SPWD's theoretical basis. Singular value velocity appears naturally in Dyson Brownian motion dynamics.
competitive_threat_update.mitigation: Cite as theoretical support, not as competition.

competitive_threat_update.reason: Unchanged from Iter 6. Provides the stability framework extended by Theorems 1-2.
competitive_threat_update.mitigation: Position as extension. 7/7 empirical confirmations are decisive.

competitive_threat_update.reason: Unchanged from Iter 6. Establishes rho-dynamics foundation.
competitive_threat_update.mitigation: State-feedback (PMP-WD) vs. feedforward (AdamC) distinction. Run AdamC as baseline.

recommendations_summary: 1. SPWD (8/10): Proceed as backup candidate. Must explicitly contrast with AlphaDecay (NeurIPS 2025) in Related Work: static PL_Alpha_Hill (AlphaDecay) vs. dynamic rank velocity d(stable_rank)/dt (SPWD). This is the single most important differentiation task for the backup candidate.

2. Theorem 3 RG derivation (8/10): Proceed as Remark 3.1 / Appendix. Present as 'independent validation from a different mathematical tradition.' The convergence of PMP and RG derivations to the same functional form is a genuine novelty point that strengthens the paper's theoretical depth.

3. Proposition 1 alignment noise (7/10): Proceed. Frame as a design constraint derived from SimiGrad's empirical observations, applied specifically to WD adaptation at b=128. Quantify CV threshold. This converts the Contrarian's challenge into a positive contribution.
4. No new threats to Theorems 1-2 (Iter 6 scores maintained). Competitive landscape for core contributions is stable.

5. Overall paper novelty assessment: HIGH. Seven distinct contributions with scores 7-9/10. The combination of formal theory (3 theorems + 1 proposition), dual derivation, and two novel algorithms (PMP-WD, SPWD) provides strong differentiation.
